brand.title: ESource AI Group (Production)
brand.subtitle: Knowledge Check — CTFS for Account Executives: From Discovery to Deal

questions.question: According to the course, what are the five financial functions that CTFS unifies on a single platform?
choices: Budgeting, contracting, forecasting, site payments, and participant payments
Budgeting, invoicing, forecasting, vendor payments, and compliance reporting
Grant management, contract negotiation, cash flow analysis, payment processing, and audit trails
Protocol budgeting, site recruitment, payment tracking, vendor management, and financial reconciliation
questions.feedback_correct: Exactly right—these five unified functions on shared data are the core differentiator of CTFS.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The correct answer focuses on the five specific financial functions that CTFS brings together. Review Slide 5 for the one-sentence definition of what CTFS does.

questions.question: What is the largest benchmark dataset that gives GrantPlan its competitive advantage in budget accuracy?
choices: 22 million negotiated data points from FDA submissions
38 million negotiated data points
51 million historical trial budgets from top 20 pharma
15 billion payment records across all geographies
questions.feedback_correct: That's correct—38 million negotiated data points is the industry-leading dataset no competitor comes close to.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The specific dataset size is 38 million negotiated data points, which powers GrantPlan's investigator grant budgeting. This differentiates CTFS on budget accuracy.

questions.question: Which proof point from the course demonstrates that the market has already adopted CTFS technologies at scale?
choices: Five hundred ten thousand plus budgets delivered across all trial phases
Two hundred plus geographies where CTFS operates
Fifty-eight percent of sponsor executives already use our technologies for clinical trial financial management
Ninety percent of the top 20 pharma companies use our technologies
questions.feedback_correct: Perfect—this Frost and Sullivan stat from May 2025 is particularly powerful because it shows peers have already moved, which is compelling social proof in discovery.
questions.feedback_incorrect: While all the options are real proof points, the 58% sponsor executive stat is the one specifically called out as most powerful for showing market directional choice in buyer conversations.

questions.question: What is Rachel's primary pain point, and what does she need to hear from an AE?
choices: Her pain is system fragmentation, and she needs an end-to-end platform story with global multicurrency support
Her pain is high maintenance cost, and she needs architectural specifics like API-first and cloud-native design
Her pain is cash flow unpredictability, and she needs predictability through automated invoicing and real-time payment visibility
Her pain is that AI pilots stall before scale, and she needs an orchestration layer connecting multiple AI agents
questions.feedback_correct: Exactly—Rachel is the Site Operations Director running lean, and predictable cash flow is her critical need.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Rachel is a Site Operations Director, not a Clinical Operations Director or IT leader. Her specific pain is cash flow uncertainty, and the solution she values is predictability through automation and visibility.

questions.question: According to the course, what is agentic AI in plain language terms?
choices: Machine learning that predicts trial outcomes based on historical data patterns
Software that perceives data, takes action, and completes multi-step workflows without a human triggering each step
Artificial intelligence agents that work independently without any oversight or human review
An API-based system that connects multiple clinical trial management tools automatically
questions.feedback_correct: That's the exact definition provided in the course—clean, jargon-free, and ready to use in any discovery conversation.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The definition focuses on software that acts autonomously across multi-step workflows without human triggering. Review Slide 14 for the plain-language explanation designed for non-technical buyers.

questions.question: When facing a real buying committee with both Gilbert and Reena present, what should the AE do first?
choices: Start with GrantPlan's 38 million data points, since that appeals to both operational and IT leaders equally
Lead with API-first architecture to establish credibility with the IT decision-maker before anyone else speaks
Start with the shared pain—fragmentation and the cost of delay—that every buyer in that room feels
Ask each person individually what their primary concern is before giving any platform information
questions.feedback_correct: Correct—opening with shared pain creates common ground, then you branch to individual value drivers as the conversation develops.
questions.feedback_incorrect: While personalization matters, the strategic move is to unite the room around a shared problem first, then differentiate value by persona as the conversation progresses.

questions.question: What is the Total Net Value formula taught in this course, and which pillar often produces the most immediate impact for a Phase III trial?
choices: Revenue Uplift + Cost Savings - Solution Cost; Cost Savings is typically largest
Revenue Uplift + Cost Savings - Solution Cost; Revenue Uplift from avoiding trial delays is typically largest
Implementation Cost - Annual Savings + Efficiency Gains; Revenue Uplift is typically largest
Cost Savings + Compliance Benefits - Licensing Fees; Cost Savings is typically largest
questions.feedback_correct: Exactly right—the formula is Revenue Uplift + Cost Savings - Solution Cost, and in Phase III trials, the delay cost ($55,000 per day) often drives the biggest number.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The Total Net Value formula has three pillars: Revenue Uplift, Cost Savings, and Solution Cost. For late-stage trials specifically, the revenue impact from avoiding delays tends to be the largest component.

questions.question: When Reena raises an integration concern about her complex tech stack, what is the appropriate counter-response from the AE?
choices: Offer to build custom middleware to connect CTFS to any existing system she currently uses
Ask if that pilot had an enterprise orchestration layer, then position CTFS as infrastructure that enables integration rather than requiring it
Question whether standards-based integration with EDC, CTMS, and ERP is truly needed, and suggest CTFS can replace those systems instead
Explain that API-first, cloud-native, and GxP/GDPR/HIPAA compliance mean CTFS works with any stack without custom work
questions.feedback_correct: Perfect—this response directly addresses Reena's architectural concerns by confirming standards-based integration exists and compliance is built in, reducing her implementation risk.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Reena's objection is about integration complexity and implementation risk. The answer focuses on the architectural features (API-first, cloud-native, compliance) and standards-based integration that make her concern addressable without custom work.

questions.question: For which persona is the Q4 2025 sandbox specifically framed as a chance to validate integration against their real tech stack with zero production risk?
choices: Gilbert, because he needs to see Site Payments running against real workflow scenarios
Andres, because he needs proof that AI infrastructure actually scales beyond a pilot
Reena, because she needs an architecture review in a safe environment without disrupting existing systems
Rachel, because she needs the fastest path to predictable payments without operational disruption
questions.feedback_correct: Correct—for Reena, the sandbox is positioned as an architecture review with no production data risk, directly addressing her concern about system disruption.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Each persona receives a different framing of the sandbox. Reena, the VP of IT Clinical, values the chance to validate integration safely. Review Slide 26 for how each buyer type should hear the sandbox opportunity.

questions.question: What is the instructor's final action item for an AE before their next real deal conversation?
choices: Memorize all four persona pain points and proof points verbatim so you can recite them in discovery
Practice the Total Net Value calculator with a real account from your pipeline, map the buyer to a persona, and open the discovery conversation this week
Wait until you have perfected your pitch and can answer every possible objection before contacting any prospect
Schedule a full platform training with your sales engineer before attempting any discovery call
questions.feedback_correct: Exactly—the course emphasizes moving to action immediately with real accounts, not waiting for perfection.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The instructor's guidance is to pick a real account, map it to a persona, and start the discovery conversation this week. The tools and frameworks are ready; applying them in practice is the next step.
